title: Dative Case — The Receiver
description: You know Nominative (the doer) and Accusative (the thing acted on). The Dative marks the person something is done FOR or TO — the indirect object. It's the third case, and it unlocks natural German.

lessons.title: What Is the Dative? (To Whom?)
lessons.subtitle: The indirect object — the receiver of the direct object.
lessons.hook: 'I give the book to the man.' There are two objects hiding here: the book (what you give — Accusative) and the man (who receives it — Dative). English uses the word 'to'; German uses the Dative case instead. Once you see this split, whole sentences click into place.
lessons.explain: The Dative case marks the indirect object — the person or thing that RECEIVES the direct object, usually answering 'to whom?' or 'for whom?'. In 'Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch' (I give the man the book), 'das Buch' is the direct object (Accusative) and 'dem Mann' is the indirect object (Dative). The typical order in German is: subject – verb – dative – accusative. Verbs like geben (to give), zeigen (to show), and schicken (to send) very often take both an accusative and a dative object.

examples.german: Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch.
examples.english: I give the man the book.

examples.german: Sie zeigt der Frau den Weg.
examples.english: She shows the woman the way.

examples.german: Wir schicken dem Kind ein Geschenk.
examples.english: We send the child a present.

vocabulary.memoryHook: Stem-changer e→i: du gibst, er gibt. Classic dative verb (give TO someone).
Regular verb. Send something to someone → dative receiver.
das Geschenk — from schenken (to give as a gift).
der Weg → den Weg as a direct object (Accusative masculine).

exercises.prompt: In 'I give the man the book', which part is the Dative (indirect object)?
exercises.explanation: The man RECEIVES the book, so 'the man' is the indirect object → Dative (dem Mann). 'The book' is the thing being given → Accusative.

exercises.prompt: The Dative usually answers which question?
exercises.explanation: Dative = 'to whom / for whom'. Nominative = who does it, Accusative = what is acted on, Genitive = whose.

exercises.prompt: What is the usual German order of objects?
exercises.explanation: With two noun objects, German normally puts the Dative (receiver) before the Accusative (thing): 'Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch.'

lessons.title: Dative Articles — dem, der, dem, den
lessons.subtitle: How der/die/das and ein/eine change in the Dative.
lessons.hook: In the Accusative, only the masculine changed. The Dative is bolder: every gender shifts, and the plural even adds an -n to the noun. But there's a clean table to memorize, and after that it's pure pattern recognition.
lessons.explain: Definite articles in the Dative: der → dem (masculine), die → der (feminine), das → dem (neuter), die → den (plural, and the noun gets an extra -n: den Kindern). Indefinite: ein → einem (m), eine → einer (f), ein → einem (n). Notice masculine and neuter share 'dem' / 'einem' again, and the feminine 'der' looks exactly like the masculine Nominative 'der' — context tells them apart. Memorize dem-der-dem-den and you own the Dative.

examples.english: The car belongs to the woman.

vocabulary.memoryHook: helfen ALWAYS takes the Dative: helfen + dem/der/mir/dir. Stem-changer e→i: du hilfst.
gehören + Dative: it belongs TO someone. Das Handy gehört dem Mann.
danken + Dative. 'Ich danke dir' — thank you (literally 'to you').

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: The phone belongs to the man.
vocabulary.memoryHook: A German 'false friend': das Handy = mobile phone (not 'handy').
Dative plural: den Kindern (die → den AND add -n to the noun).

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich helfe ___ Frau.' (I help the woman.)
exercises.hint: helfen takes Dative; Frau is feminine.
exercises.explanation: Feminine Dative: die → der. helfen requires the Dative, so 'der Frau'. (Yes, it looks like masculine Nominative 'der' — context disambiguates.)

exercises.prompt: What happens to a plural noun in the Dative?
options: the article becomes den and the noun adds -n
exercises.correctAnswer: the article becomes den and the noun adds -n
exercises.explanation: Dative plural: die → den, and the noun takes an extra -n (den Kindern, den Männern). This -n is a signature marker of the Dative plural.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Das Handy gehört ___ Mann.' (The phone belongs to the man.)
exercises.hint: gehören takes Dative; Mann is masculine.
exercises.explanation: Masculine Dative: der → dem. gehören requires the Dative, so 'dem Mann'.

lessons.title: Dative Prepositions & Verbs
lessons.subtitle: mit, aus, zu, bei… always trigger the Dative. Learn the crew.
lessons.hook: Some prepositions are Dative magnets — whenever you use them, the noun after them MUST go Dative, no exceptions. Germans even chant them: 'aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu'. Learn that little rhythm and half your Dative decisions are automatic.
lessons.explain: These common prepositions ALWAYS take the Dative: aus (from/out of), bei (at/near), mit (with), nach (after/to), seit (since), von (from/of), zu (to). A memory chant is 'aus-bei-mit-nach-seit-von-zu'. Also, a set of verbs always take a Dative object even without a preposition: helfen (help), danken (thank), gehören (belong to), gefallen (to please/like), antworten (answer). With these, the person is always in the Dative: 'Das gefällt mir' (I like that — literally 'that pleases to me').

examples.english: I like that. (lit. that pleases to me)

vocabulary.memoryHook: Dative preposition. Also used for transport: mit dem Bus/Auto/Zug.
Dative preposition. Often contracts: zu dem → zum, zu der → zur.
Flipped logic: the thing is the subject, the person is Dative. 'X gefällt mir' = I like X.
antworten + Dative person: you answer TO someone (antworte mir).

exercises.prompt: Which case do 'mit, aus, zu, bei, von' always trigger?
exercises.explanation: These are Dative prepositions — the noun after them is always Dative. The chant 'aus-bei-mit-nach-seit-von-zu' captures the core group.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich fahre mit ___ Bus.' (I go by bus.) — der Bus is masculine
exercises.hint: mit takes Dative; masculine Dative article.
exercises.explanation: 'mit' forces the Dative, and masculine Dative is 'dem': mit dem Bus.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I like Berlin' using gefallen?
exercises.explanation: With gefallen, the liked thing is the subject and the person is Dative: 'Berlin gefällt mir' (literally: Berlin pleases to me).
